Paul's concern throughout this chapter is so practical it might surprise us: when God's people gather, can anyone actually understand what is happening? He has just finished celebrating the diversity of spiritual gifts in the church, but now he confronts a harsh reality. The Corinthians are gathering together and speaking in tongues—a legitimate gift—but doing it without interpretation, without order, and without regard for whether anyone else benefits. The result is spiritual chaos masquerading as devotion. Paul calls this what it is: unintelligible noise that builds up only the speaker's own sense of spiritual experience while leaving the church confused and outsiders thinking the assembly has lost its mind. This is a devastating rebuke wrapped in logic, because Paul is not attacking the gifts themselves—he speaks in tongues more than anyone—but he is attacking the self-centeredness that prioritizes personal experience over the community's edification and over the clarity of the gospel itself.

What makes this rebuke so piercing is that Paul forces the Corinthians to see their worship through the eyes of someone else. He makes them imagine an unbeliever walking into their gathering and hearing only ecstatic utterances they cannot understand. What would that person conclude about Christ? What would they learn about God's kingdom? By contrast, when prophecy operates—clear, intelligible proclamation that points people toward truth—an unbeliever experiences conviction and sees that God truly is among them. This is not about style preferences or personal preferences. This is about the fundamental purpose of gathering: to edify one another and to display the wisdom and presence of God to a watching world. Paul is insisting that love—the very thing he commanded in verse 1—cannot coexist with indifference to whether others understand or are helped.

But notice what Paul does not do: he does not forbid tongues-speaking. Instead, he establishes guardrails. If someone speaks in a tongue, there must be an interpreter. No more than two or three should speak, and it must be in turn, not all at once. Women should refrain from public teaching in this particular congregation (whether this addresses a specific local problem or reflects a broader principle remains debated among faithful interpreters). Prophets should speak in turn, and others should weigh what is said. Everything must be orderly. The thread running through all of this is submission—not rebellion against genuine spiritual gifts, but submission to the reality that worship belongs to God and to His people together, not to individuals seeking their own spiritual highs. Paul ends where he began: be eager for prophecy, do not forbid tongues, but do everything decently and in order. Maturity, he insists, means knowing when to speak and when to be silent, when to exercise your gift and when to hold back for the sake of the whole body.

The weight of this chapter falls on us too. What drives the way we worship and gather? Do we genuinely care whether others are edified, whether the gospel is made clear, whether the stranger walking in meets the Lord through what they see and hear? Or do we, like Corinth, sometimes prioritize what feels personally meaningful while ignoring the effect on those around us? Paul's command to love is not sentimental. It means asking hard questions about our choices in worship, teaching, and witness. It means sometimes surrendering what we want for the sake of clarity and community. It means recognizing that our spiritual experience is not the measure of faithfulness—the building up of God's people is.
